original_abstract:
This paper presents the results of a 40-month continuous test campaign evaluating the degradation management of CESAR1—an aqueous blend of 2-amino-2-methylpropan-1-ol (AMP) and piperazine (PZ)—at a lignite-fired power plant pilot facility in Niederaussem. The study challenges conventional industrial guidelines that advocate for maintaining maximum "solvent cleanliness" to minimize amine consumption. Three distinct management strategies were investigated: ion exchange for the removal of ionic contaminants, active carbon adsorption for trace element and color removal, and flue gas pretreatment with thiosulfate/sulfite to reduce $NO_2$ levels.

The findings reveal that standard indicators, such as solvent color and metal concentration, do not reliably predict degradation rates for CESAR1. Notably, while active carbon effectively brightened the solvent and captured iron and nickel, it had no discernible impact on the accumulation of degradation products. Conversely, removing anionic compounds via ion exchange unexpectedly doubled the degradation rate, suggesting that certain accumulated contaminants may actually serve as ligands that deactivate metal catalysts. Furthermore, $NO_2$ removal did not significantly reduce oxidative degradation or nitrosamine formation, though thermal treatment at $130^\circ\text{C}$ proved effective for nitrosamine control without compromising solvent stability. The results underscore the necessity of solvent-specific management strategies and caution against excessive cleaning, which can lead to unnecessary waste and efficiency losses. Over 28,000 operating hours, CESAR1 demonstrated high stability and linear degradation, proving far more robust than traditional monoethanolamine (MEA) systems.
humanized_abstract:
The paper presents the results of the CESAR1 test campaign performed in the context of the CESAR1 project over a period of 40 months. The test facility is located at a lignite-fired power plant pilot demonstration facility in Niederaussem. It is aimed at demonstrating the degradation management of an aqueous blend of 2-amino-2-methylpropan-1-ol (AMP) and piperazine (PZ) in a working power plant and at refuting the general industrial practice of operating processes with the maximum possible “solvent cleanliness” in order to avoid high amine consumption. In order to this end, three processes were tested within the framework of the project: ion exchange for the elimination of ions, active carbon adsorption for elimination of trace elements and colours and flue gas pretreatment by means of thiosulfate/sulfite for $NO_2$ reduction.

We find that standard measures such as solvent color and metal content do not correlate with CESAR1 degradation rate. We also show that using active carbon to decolor the solvent and remove iron and nickel does not influence degradation product formation. The removal of anionic species by exchange media surprisingly increases the CESAR1 catalytic activity by a factor of two. This indicates that a portion of the accumulation of contaminants might act as ligands and therefore inactivate the catalytic centers. The removal of $NO_2$ by $130^{\circ}\text{C}$ treatment does not affect oxidative degradation and the formation of nitrosation products. The CESAR1 performance and stability over more than 28 000 operating hours in a working pilot demonstrates that it is much more stable and that it degrades much more linearly than MEA. The excess of cleaning can avoid no benefit and produce only unnecessary wastewater and consumptions.

document.output:

document.input:
This paper presents the results of a 40-month continuous test campaign evaluating the degradation management of CESAR1—an aqueous blend of 2-amino-2-methylpropan-1-ol (AMP) and piperazine (PZ)—at a lignite-fired power plant pilot facility in Niederaussem. The study challenges conventional industrial guidelines that advocate for maintaining maximum "solvent cleanliness" to minimize amine consumption. Three distinct management strategies were investigated: ion exchange for the removal of ionic contaminants, active carbon adsorption for trace element and color removal, and flue gas pretreatment with thiosulfate/sulfite to reduce $NO_2$ levels.

The findings reveal that standard indicators, such as solvent color and metal concentration, do not reliably predict degradation rates for CESAR1. Notably, while active carbon effectively brightened the solvent and captured iron and nickel, it had no discernible impact on the accumulation of degradation products. Conversely, removing anionic compounds via ion exchange unexpectedly doubled the degradation rate, suggesting that certain accumulated contaminants may actually serve as ligands that deactivate metal catalysts. Furthermore, $NO_2$ removal did not significantly reduce oxidative degradation or nitrosamine formation, though thermal treatment at $130^\circ\text{C}$ proved effective for nitrosamine control without compromising solvent stability. The results underscore the necessity of solvent-specific management strategies and caution against excessive cleaning, which can lead to unnecessary waste and efficiency losses. Over 28,000 operating hours, CESAR1 demonstrated high stability and linear degradation, proving far more robust than traditional monoethanolamine (MEA) systems.
